item.title: A refactoração é uma tarefa complexa
item.author: Ortiz de Arcanjo António David
item.content:encoded: A refactoração, ao contrário do que a teoria sugere, é uma tarefa muito complexa. Os livros e artigos costumam mostrar exemplos de funções simples e classes pequenas, mas o mundo real está muito distante dessa visão superficial. Projectos reais carregam uma história — um conjunto de decisões que foram tomadas ao longo do tempo e que levaram o código ao estado actual. É comum pensar que o projecto está desorganizado ou que não segue boas práticas, mas muitas dessas decisões foram influenciadas pela pressão do negócio e pela necessidade de entregar rápido.Em projectos reais, é comum encontrar arquivos com milhares de linhas. O que realmente ajuda é entender os padrões, perceber a intenção original e agir com cuidado. Refactorar é muito diferente da teoria. Não se trata apenas de mudar o nome de uma classe, struct ou arquivo — há muitas variáveis envolvidas.Identificar código repetidoMover abstrações para helpersFazer refactorações pequenas e seguras, em vez de grandes mudanças de uma só vez Em outras linguagens, seria comum dividir grandes arquivos em várias classes. Mas em Go, a estrutura da linguagem facilita bastante esse processo.A linguagem Go ajuda muito na refactoração:Funções a nível de pacoteStructs a nível de pacoteFacilidade em isolar helpers, mesmo sem classes Além disso, usar uma boa IDE faz toda a diferença — facilita navegar, testar e refactorar com segurança.Refactorar é mais sobre compreender o contexto do código do que apenas reescrever funções. É um processo de respeito pela história do projecto e de construção de algo melhor, passo a passo.

item.title: Container Networking Demystified: A Deep Dive into Linux Network Namespaces
item.author: Ajinkya Singh
item.content:encoded:
🚀 I'm Building My Own Container Runtime!This is part of a complete series where I'm building - a container runtime from scratch. Check it out on GitHub!I'm sharing everything I learn while building my own container runtimeMost concepts come from videos, documentation, and LLM-assisted learning (for educational purposes)Focus: Understanding through practice - raw Linux commands and practical implementation: When building your own container, DON'T copy code from sources - it kills the fun! Write it yourself, break things, debug, and learn.Deep understanding of how containers really workMaster low-level Linux conceptsLearn by doing, not just readingIt's incredibly fun when things finally click!
Understanding How Docker and Kubernetes Actually Work Under the Hood Have you ever wondered how Docker containers remain isolated yet can communicate with each other and the internet? The answer lies in a powerful Linux kernel feature called . In this comprehensive guide, we'll unravel the mysteries of container networking by building our own container network from scratch.
The Apartment Building Analogy Think of your Linux host as an apartment building, and network namespaces as individual apartments within that building:: Each apartment (namespace) has its own utilities, locks, and living space: The building manager (host) can access all apartments: Apartments can be connected through hallways and shared spaces when neededThis is exactly how containers work—each one gets its own isolated network stack, completely separate from the host and other containers.
Part 1: Creating Your First Network Namespace Let's start by creating an isolated network environment. First, verify you have the required tools: ip iptables apt-get update apt-get iproute2 iptables bridge-utils
Exploring Network Isolation
ip show ip netns add production
ip netns list
ip netns production ip show : The namespace is completely isolated. It has no access to your host's network interfaces, routing tables, or firewall rules.
Bringing the Namespace to Life ip netns production ip lo up
ip netns production ping 2 127.0.0.1 ip netns production ping 2 8.8.8.8
Part 2: Connecting Namespaces with Virtual Cables To connect namespaces, we use (virtual ethernet pairs). Think of them as virtual network cables with two ends.
Creating a Point-to-Point Connection ip netns add frontend ip netns add backend
ip add veth-front veth peer name veth-back
ip show | veth
ip veth-front netns frontend ip veth-back netns backend
ip show | veth ip netns frontend ip addr add 10.0.1.10/24 dev veth-front ip netns frontend ip veth-front up ip netns frontend ip lo up
ip netns backend ip addr add 10.0.1.20/24 dev veth-back ip netns backend ip veth-back up ip netns backend ip lo up
ip netns frontend ping 3 10.0.1.20 ip netns backend python3 &
ip netns frontend curl http://10.0.1.20:8080
Part 3: Scaling with Linux Bridges Direct veth connections only work for pairs of namespaces. For multiple containers, we need a —essentially a virtual network switch.
Creating a Bridge Network ip add cloud-bridge bridge ip cloud-bridge up ip addr add 172.20.0.1/24 dev cloud-bridge
i 1 2 3ip netns add appip add vethveth peer name ethip veth master cloud-bridge ip veth up
ip eth netns appip netns app ip addr add 172.20.0.i+10/24 dev ethip netns app ip eth up ip netns app ip lo up
i+10
Testing Full Mesh Connectivity ip netns app1 ping 2 172.20.0.12
ip netns app2 ping 2 172.20.0.13
ip netns app3 ping 2 172.20.0.11
Part 4: Internet Connectivity with NAT Now for the crucial part—giving our namespaces access to the external world. This requires three components:: Tell namespace where to send external traffic: Allow host to forward packetsNAT (Network Address Translation): Replace private IPs with host's public IPip netns app1 ip route add default via 172.20.0.1
sysctl net.ipv4.ip_forward1
iptables nat POSTROUTING 172.20.0.0/24 MASQUERADE
ip netns app1 ping 3 8.8.8.8 ip netns app1 curl http://api.ipify.org
Understanding NAT Packet Flow Here's what happens when a namespace accesses the internet:1. Namespace sends packet: Source: 172.20.0.11 (private) Destination: 8.8.8.8
2. Host receives packet on bridge: Routing table: "Forward to eth0"
3. NAT happens in POSTROUTING: BEFORE: src=172.20.0.11 AFTER: src=<host's public IP>
4. Packet leaves via eth0: Now has valid public source IP Internet routers accept it
5. Reply returns: Connection tracking remembers translation Destination NAT: dst=<host IP> → dst=172.20.0.11 Packet forwarded back to namespace
Part 5: Exposing Services with Port Forwarding To make services in namespaces accessible from the outside world, we use port forwarding.ip netns app1 python3 &
iptables nat PREROUTING tcp 8080 DNAT 172.20.0.11:80
iptables FORWARD tcp 172.20.0.11 80 ACCEPT
curl http://localhost:8080 iptables nat PREROUTING tcp 8081 DNAT 172.20.0.11:80
iptables nat PREROUTING tcp 8082 DNAT 172.20.0.12:80
iptables nat PREROUTING tcp 3000 DNAT 172.20.0.13:3000
Part 6: Network Security and Isolation iptables FORWARD 172.20.0.11 172.20.0.12 DROP iptables FORWARD 172.20.0.12 172.20.0.11 DROP
ip netns app1 ping 2 1 172.20.0.12 ip netns app1 ping 2 172.20.0.13
Creating a Secure Database Network ip add secure-bridge bridge ip addr add 10.10.0.1/24 dev secure-bridge ip secure-bridge up
ip netns add database
ip add veth-db veth peer name eth-db ip veth-db master secure-bridge ip veth-db up ip eth-db netns database ip netns database ip addr add 10.10.0.2/24 dev eth-db ip netns database ip eth-db up ip netns database ip lo up
iptables FORWARD 172.20.0.11 10.10.0.2 tcp 3306 ACCEPT iptables FORWARD 10.10.0.2 DROP
Critical Concept: Why Bridges Matter (Point-to-Point):Host has one end of veth pairThey can only talk to each otherAdding more containers requires more veth pairsNo automatic forwarding between containers (Network Switch):Bridge acts as virtual switchAll containers connect to bridge via veth pairsHost connects to bridge with single interfaceBridge handles MAC learning and forwarding automaticallyScales to unlimited containersThis is why Docker creates the bridge—it's the virtual switch connecting all containers!
Practical Exercise: Three-Tier Application Let's build a complete three-tier application network:ip add frontend-bridge bridge ip addr add 192.168.10.1/24 dev frontend-bridge ip frontend-bridge up
ip add backend-bridge bridge ip addr add 192.168.20.1/24 dev backend-bridge ip backend-bridge up
ip add database-bridge bridge ip addr add 192.168.30.1/24 dev database-bridge ip database-bridge up
ip netns add web-server ip add veth-web veth peer name eth-web ip veth-web master frontend-bridge ip veth-web up ip eth-web netns web-server ip netns web-server ip addr add 192.168.10.10/24 dev eth-web ip netns web-server ip eth-web up ip netns web-server ip lo up ip netns web-server ip route add default via 192.168.10.1
ip netns add api-server ip add veth-api veth peer name eth-api ip veth-api master backend-bridge ip veth-api up ip eth-api netns api-server ip netns api-server ip addr add 192.168.20.10/24 dev eth-api ip netns api-server ip eth-api up ip netns api-server ip lo up
ip netns add db-server ip add veth-db veth peer name eth-db ip veth-db master database-bridge ip veth-db up ip eth-db netns db-server ip netns db-server ip addr add 192.168.30.10/24 dev eth-db ip netns db-server ip eth-db up ip netns db-server ip lo up
sysctl net.ipv4.ip_forward1
iptables FORWARD 192.168.10.0/24 192.168.20.0/24 ACCEPT iptables FORWARD 192.168.20.0/24 192.168.10.0/24 ACCEPT
iptables FORWARD 192.168.20.0/24 192.168.30.0/24 ACCEPT iptables FORWARD 192.168.30.0/24 192.168.20.0/24 ACCEPT
iptables FORWARD 192.168.10.0/24 192.168.30.0/24 DROP
iptables nat POSTROUTING 192.168.10.0/24 MASQUERADE
iptables nat PREROUTING tcp 80 DNAT 192.168.10.10:80
ns ip netns list | ip netns delete bridge cloud-bridge frontend-bridge backend-bridge database-bridge secure-bridgeip show &>/dev/nullip delete iptables nat iptables FORWARD
provide complete network isolation—the foundation of container networking act as virtual ethernet cables connecting namespaces together work like virtual network switches, enabling scalable multi-container networks allows private container IPs to access the internet by masquerading behind the host's public IP exposes containerized services to external networks provides powerful firewall capabilities for network security and isolation: Every concept we explored is exactly how Docker, Kubernetes, and other container platforms work under the hood

item.title: Sometimes small ideas teach you the biggest lessons.
item.author: Satyam Shree
item.content:encoded:
🧠 Go-URL: Building a Lightweight, Scalable URL Shortener in Go Sometimes small projects end up teaching you the biggest lessons. started as a simple weekend idea — “let’s build a basic URL shortener in Go.” But as I started designing it, I realized it was a perfect opportunity to explore , , , and — all within a compact, real-world problem space.The goal was simple: with:And do it all using technologies I love working with: , , , and .Core backend logic, API server, concurrency handlingFast caching for redirect lookupsPersistent storage for original and shortened URLsDeployment, load balancing, auto-scalingContainerization for portabilityThe API is built using package and structured around clean architecture principles — keeping , , and decoupled.This design allows horizontal scaling — multiple instances can run behind a load balancer with no shared state.URL redirection is read-heavy. Instead of querying PostgreSQL for every hit, Redis caches the mappings:short_url -> original_url Whenever a new short URL is created, it’s stored in both Redis and PostgreSQL. On lookups, if Redis misses, the record is fetched from the DB and re-cached — a classic pattern.
3. PostgreSQL for Durability Redis is blazing fast but volatile. To ensure persistence, PostgreSQL stores all URL mappings permanently, complete with timestamps and access logs.A syncs write operations asynchronously to maintain API responsiveness.
4. Kubernetes for Scalability I containerized the service with Docker and deployed it on . across pods based on CPU/memory metrics without downtime This setup ensures Go-URL can handle sudden traffic spikes smoothly.
🧠 Key Challenges and Learnings Balancing cache and consistency Designing the Redis sync flow without introducing stale data was tricky. A hybrid TTL-based strategy worked best. Async syncs without blocking requests in Go to offload DB writes, improving response times significantly. Added basic logging and metrics using Go’s and structured logs — it helped a lot during debugging and load testing. Watching pods auto-scale under simulated load was incredibly satisfying — a real-world validation of clean design. After load testing with and , the results were impressive: Median response time: (with Redis warm) Sustained across multiple instances Zero downtime during redeployments via Kubernetes rolling updates Go-URL reminded me that even the simplest systems can be if you think about them right.How helps with maintainability. Why are crucial for scalability. How simplifies deployment complexity. And most importantly — how small design decisions impact performance at scale.If you’re into backend systems, Go, or distributed design — I’d love to hear how would scale or extend this system further.
#golang #backend #systemdesign #redis #kubernetes #scalability #learningbybuilding

item.title: My journey to Go
item.author: Ruxo Zheng
item.content:encoded: I'm now starting to look at the Go language seriously, whereas before I just thought it was an "Indy" language. In the past month, I've had the chance to work with a team that uses Go, which led me to read up on it. I stumbled upon one of its core ideas: Go doesn't have "async" programming (it has green threads—logical threads that handle concurrency via software through context switching). But Go is actually an asynchronous language by default! (It's comparable to every single function in Go using async/await.) The interesting part is that Go makes all the code look synchronous (but it handles the async parts where needed automatically). Personally, I really like this code style, but people who haven't read the specs might misunderstand and think Go is a single-threaded application, much like JS or Python.If anyone has tried Haskell's IO monad, you'll find it's the same concept: you wrap a function (whether sync or async) with an IO type, and it ultimately makes the code look synchronous even though it can be both sync and async. In the past, I tried to apply this concept to several languages (C#, F#, TypeScript) but gave up because it involved a lot of boilerplate, and the resulting syntax was very "alien" (for anyone who wants to try the IO monad with JS/TS, check out https://effect.website/). So I never used it seriously. But Go embeds this idea through Go routines, whether by coincidence or not.Another thing that made me uninterested in Go before: over ten years ago, I read about the language's origin, wondering why it was designed to be so "indy." I remember one interview quote from a designer: "Go is a language designed for junior developers to help them catch up with a codebase quickly." This idea led Go to cut out many abstract concepts (even generic types!). I had tried Go 2-3 times before but got annoyed because the lack of abstract ideas, especially generics, resulted in a lot of code duplication.But recently, I had the chance to manage a team of Chula graduates (honors, new grads). The team uses C++, but they were using it at a very basic level, and some didn't understand core concepts like RAII. (Oh, and this made me go back and read the C++11 specs and discover that C++ has had the 'move' concept for over a decade! At first, I thought Rust introduced this idea.)So I set up a 3-hour course to teach them about C++ memory pitfalls and RAII and found that... 3 hours was not nearly enough. And honestly, to use this stuff correctly, it takes months of practice and understanding, especially since C++ has so many nuances if you want to write truly idiomatic code.It was only this past month that I finally had an "aha" moment about the Go designer's words. Go wants devs to get up to speed quickly while still remaining low-level enough to write system programming—even though it has mostly become an application language now.

item.title: Decorators: The Programming World’s Fancy Wrappers (And Why Your Code Needs a Makeover)
item.author: Dev TNG
item.content:encoded:
Decorators: The Classiest Duct Tape in Programming Decorators are like the duct tape of programming—except way classier.They let you wrap functions in extra logic without rewriting them, saving you from spaghetti code and existential dread. Python's got the swag, JavaScript's got decorators (if you're cool with TypeScript), and Go? Well, Go just hands you a function and says, Here's why you should care: cleaner code, less repetition, and the sweet satisfaction of looking like a pro.
Why Do Decorators Exist? (AKA "Who Hurt You, Code?") 1. The DRY Principle: Because Copy-Pasting is So 2025: Write once, reuse everywhere.: Nobody wants to update the same logging code in 50 places. Decorators let you slap that logic on top like a sticker.: We tested this on a legacy project. Refactored 300 lines into 3 decorators. Saved 2 hours and 3 existential crises.2. Separation of Concerns: Keep Your Code's Drama in Check: Logic stays modular. Auth, logging, and retries don't need to live inside your function.: Mixing concerns is like putting ketchup on ice cream—technically possible, but : In our benchmarks, decorated functions were 18% easier to debug (yes, we measured, barely).3. Readability: Because Your Future Self Will Thank You: is clearer than a nested try-catch hellscape.: Code should read like a story, not a ransom note.: Python's decorators are the closest thing to magic spells in programming.
How to Decorate Like a Pro (Without Looking Like a Tryhard) : One line = memoization, logging, or retries. No sweat.JavaScript: TypeScript's Secret Sauce: Angular and NestJS devs nod approvingly.Go: "Hold My Beer" Edition: No syntax sugar, just raw power. Like a gym membership for your functions.: Because Rubyists love metaprogramming like cats love boxes.
When NOT to Use Decorators (Yes, There's a Dark Side)
⚠️ Performance-critical code – Your coworker.
FAQ: Your Burning Questions, Answered Q: Are decorators just functions in disguise?: Yes, but with better PR. They're syntactic sugar for higher-order functions. Python and JS just make them look fancy.Q: Can I stack decorators?: Absolutely. Python lets you do . JS/TS too. Go? You're on your own, buddy.Q: Why doesn't Ruby have decorators?: Because Rubyists prefer metaprogramming black magic. See: , , and .Q: Will decorators make me a better developer?: No, but they'll make you like one. Use them wisely.
The One Quotable Truth About Decorators "Decorators are the programming equivalent of a tuxedo: not always necessary, but when you need them, nothing else will do."– Some Wise Dev (Probably Us)Next time someone asks why your code is so clean, just say, Then walk away mysteriously.P.S. If you're still copy-pasting the same code across 50 functions, we can't help you. But decorators can.

item.title: 🎃 Contribute to a Go REST API Boilerplate — Perfect for Hacktoberfest Beginners!
item.author: Vahid Vahedi
item.content:encoded: I’ve built a Go REST API boilerplate to help developers learn how to structure and build production-ready APIs quickly and cleanly.For Hacktoberfest 2025, I’ve added several beginner-friendly issues labeled:hacktoberfest good first issue 🔗 Repository 👉 github.com/vahiiiid/go-rest-api-boilerplateTopics: Go, REST, Middleware, Docker, Testing🧩 How You Can Contribute You can:Pick an existing issue (each is well-described with clear hints and examples) Or create your own discussion or issue if you have an idea to improve the project! All issues are AI-friendly, meaning you can use tools like ChatGPT or GitHub Copilot for guidance while implementing your changes 🤖 Each issue includes: 📋 Clear acceptance criteria 💡 Code examples and hints 🤖 AI-friendly (ChatGPT/Copilot welcome!)🧭 How to Get Started Fork the repo Create a new branch Open a pull request 🎉 Even small PRs count — every contribution helps improve the boilerplate for future developers!💬 Let’s Connect If you’d like to discuss ideas or need help, feel free to:Comment on an issue Start a discussion in the repo Or message me here on DEV.to! Happy coding, and have an awesome Hacktoberfest! 🎃

item.title: Email Validation and SMTP Handshake With Go
item.author: Joy Biswas
item.content:encoded:
I was implementing a signup endpoint for an API where users can register via email. There are many ways to verify an email address. You could use a popular library like to check if the input is in an email format. However, this doesn't confirm if the email address actually exists or if its domain can receive mail.If you prefer not to use regex or an external package, Go's standard library offers the package to validate an email address format.This approach is generally better than a simple regex validation because parses an address according to the official , which defines the standard for email format.Let's start by checking if a string has a valid email format.Example 1: A Well-Formatted but Non-Existent EmailThis code will run without an error. It passes the test because the string contains a local part (), an symbol, and a domain (). The function only cares about the format, not whether the address is real.Example 2: An Incorrect FormatIn this case, the program will panic and show the error: mail: missing '@' in address.Format validation is a good first step, but it doesn't prevent users from signing up with an address like test@nonexistent-domain.com. Our next thought might be to check the domain's DNS records.A common but flawed approach is to look up the host directly.This check for an or record is not what we need for email validation. Many people purchase domains exclusively for email services and never set up a website, so they may not have an record pointing to a web server. Letting users pass verification based on this check is a bad idea.The correct approach is to check for records. These DNS records specify the mail servers responsible for accepting email on behalf of a domain. If a domain has records, it's configured to receive email.First, let's get the domain name from the email address and use to find the records for that domain.For a valid address like , you'll see a result similar to this, confirming the domain is set up to receive mail:MX Records gmail.com: Host: gmail-smtp-in.l.google.com., Preference: 5 Host: alt1.gmail-smtp-in.l.google.com., Preference: 10 Host: alt2.gmail-smtp-in.l.google.com., Preference: 20 Host: alt3.gmail-smtp-in.l.google.com., Preference: 30 Host: alt4.gmail-smtp-in.l.google.com., Preference: 40 If you run the same code on a domain with no records, will return an error, such as lookup joybtw.com: no such host. This tells us the domain is not configured to receive email, and we should reject the address.The SMTP Handshake to Verify the MailboxNow that we know the domain is configured to receive mail, we can go one step further by asking the server if the specific mailbox (e.g., ) actually exists. We can do this by performing a partial .This process involves connecting to the mail server and simulating the first few steps of sending an email, stopping just before transmitting any actual content. The key is the command, where we ask the server if it will accept a message for our target email address. The server's response tells us whether the user likely exists.Connect to the Mail Server: We connect to the mail server with the highest priority (lowest preference number) on port 25.Start the Conversation (): We greet the server to start the conversation.Specify the Sender (): We state the sender's address.Check the Recipient (): We provide the email address to validate.
A response code starting with means "OK," and the address is likely valid. A response code like means the address does not exist.End the Conversation (): We properly close the connection without sending an email.While powerful, this method isn't foolproof: Some servers are configured with a "catch-all" policy, meaning they accept emails for address at that domain to prevent spammers from guessing valid addresses. In this case, the check will always succeed, even for non-existent mailboxes. Mail servers may use security features like greylisting or block automated checks altogether. This can lead to false negatives, where a valid address appears invalid.Here is the complete code:Here is a simple, runnable Go program that combines these validation steps.This code is for educational purposes to demonstrate the concepts of email validation. It is not production-level code. For a real-world application, you would need more robust error handling, connection timeouts, and logic to iterate through multiple MX records if the primary one fails.

item.title: Quick Guide: First 5 Steps I Give Every New Go Mentee
item.author: Evgeny Pantela
item.content:encoded: Hi Dev.to community! As a Go developer with 3 years of hands-on experience and a mentor for the past 1.5 years, having guided over 100 aspiring developers, I've noticed a pattern: new engineers often get stuck trying to master complex architectural patterns before they’ve nailed the fundamentals of quality and reliability.Master Your Environment: IDE and Tooling Setup Your toolchain must enforce Go’s best practices automatically. Don’t waste brainpower on formatting or obvious errors. GoLand or VS Code with Extensions: Pick a mature IDE (I prefer GoLand for large projects) and ensure it's configured for auto-formatting. Install goimports and golint: Integrate goimports to manage package imports automatically and ensure your code is clean and idiomatic. golint (or a modern replacement like gofmt -s) should be run on commit. The Rule: If your tooling can fix it, let it. Your focus must remain on business logic and architecture.Test Early, Test Often: The Table-Driven Way In Go, testing is a first-class citizen, not an afterthought. Adopting Table-Driven Tests (TDT) is the quickest way to write comprehensive, readable, and repeatable tests, proving test coverage is not just possible, but easy. TDT helps structure your tests logically, clearly defining inputs, expected outputs, and test case names. This quickly accelerates your understanding of edge cases.Go func CalculateDiscount(price float64, isMember bool) float64 { return price * 0.9 // 10% discount return price// Example of Table-Driven Test structure: var tests = []struct { inputPrice float64 expected float64 {"MemberDiscount", 100.0, true, 90.0}, {"NoDiscount", 100.0, false, 100.0}, {"ZeroPrice", 0.0, true, 0.0},// In a full TDT, you loop through this table.Embrace context.Context from Day One This is where Go truly shines in production environments, especially for network services. Many developers treat context.Context as optional until it's too late. Propagation is Key: Pass the context.Context as the very first argument to any function that performs I/O, handles requests, or involves timing out. This includes database calls, HTTP requests, and even internal long-running tasks. Cancellation and Timeouts: Learn to use context.WithTimeout and context.WithCancel to define boundaries for your operations. This is fundamental to building reliable, resilient microservices that don't leak resources or hang indefinitely. This practice is essential for building scalable, high-quality distributed systems—something any leader in the tech sector should master.Static Analysis: Linting for the Future Go’s compiler is fast and powerful, but it won't catch logic that is technically legal but structurally flawed. Install staticcheck: Make this part of your pre-commit hook or CI pipeline. Tools like staticcheck go beyond basic style to find logical bugs, potential performance issues, and non-idiomatic code. Why it Matters: Adherence to community standards is crucial for collaboration. It reduces the cognitive load on future team members who inherit your code, ultimately lowering business costs and increasing developer velocity.Think Small, Build Atomic: Prioritize Tasks Don't aim to build the entire system at once. This leads to burnout and scope creep. Atomic PRs/Commits: Teach yourself to break down tasks into the smallest, independently testable units. Each pull request should address one problem and should be concise. The Result is Focus: This discipline ensures your development is consistent and predictable. It minimizes merge conflicts, speeds up code review, and guarantees incremental progress—a practice that mirrors effective project management.Conclusion and Next Steps (CTA)Mastering these five fundamental steps creates a solid, reliable foundation for any Go developer. It transforms novices into reliable, effective contributors who write clean and predictable code.Found this helpful? I wrote a deeper dive on my core mentoring philosophy (using Go principles)

item.title: Go Beyond Basics: Closures, Interfaces, and Why Go Has No Inheritance
item.author: Saksham Malhotra
item.content:encoded:
I’ve already covered some of the basics of earlier. However, before diving into topics like goroutines and wait times, I think it’s important to first discuss a few key aspects of **Go **and how it’s typically used.As we already know, has closures just like javascript. The only differences are: Strong typing make closures more predictable.Go closures capture variables by reference, not by value (similar to using var in JS instead of let or const). Go has a built-in interface called . When you print a value using fmt.Println, fmt.Printf, or similar functions, Go’s formatting code internally checks:“Does this value implement the Stringer interface?”If yes then, it calls your custom String() method to get the string representation. Else it prints the default (struct fields, numeric value, etc.).Bonus: Go’s Type System Rule - Named types are distinct from their underlying types.Yes, you heard it right, no more confusion arising from same-name methods. Go’s interface satisfaction is structural and implicit.It doesn’t care which interface you meant to implement.It only cares that your type’s method set matches the interface’s method signatures.So if two interfaces have identical methods, implementing one automatically implements the other.The above code won’t compile because func (a int) is invalid in Go — you cannot attach a method to an unnamed type.
Interface values are two-part boxes A Go interface value is actually a pair of things:(dynamic type, dynamic value) Eg: (dynamic type = MyInt, dynamic value = 7)Unlike other languages, you’re not “creating an object of the interface”Instead, you’re creating a variable of interface type that stores a value (of any type) that satisfies the interface.The interface variable is like a box that can hold any value, as long as the value provides the required methods.Interface = Plug socket. Any type that fits (has the right methods) can plug in; no prior agreement required.
Methods Can Belong to Any Named Type In Go, you can attach a method to any named type, not just structs or interfaces.Now, this brings us to a question: Why do interfaces exist even though any named type can have methods?And the answer is simple, the methods attached to a type is encapsulation, but not polymorphism. What methods to a type can't do:Without any interface, you would need to write one function per type :Interfaces solve this problem: They say, I don't care about the type as long as it knows how to do this behavior:The interface just describes behavior i.e. a contract, not an implementation.Above thing works because MyType has a method Foo() with the same signature as required by A. It's not inheritance or anything else, but a contract. So, if A was :and rest of the code remain same, then it will be a compiler error, since the interface contract says to have two methods but is only assigning it one. Go’s design favors clarity, not magic. It doesn’t hide behavior behind inheritance or overloading; instead, it gives you small, powerful primitives: named types, methods, and interfaces.Interfaces in Go aren’t about hierarchy but about capability. If a type can do what’s expected, it fits. That’s why Go’s feels lightweight yet expressive.From closures that behave predictably, to interfaces that define contracts rather than family trees, it reminds us that simplicity, when designed well, is not a limitation but a superpower.

item.title: Build a Blazing-Fast TCP Server in Go: A Practical Guide
item.author: Jones Charles
item.content:encoded:
Imagine you're the air traffic controller of a bustling digital airport, guiding thousands of data packets to their destinations with precision. That's the life of a TCP server, the unsung hero behind chat apps, IoT platforms, and game servers. In this guide, we'll harness Go's superpowers to build a high-performance TCP server that handles thousands of connections with ease.This article is for developers with 1-2 years of Go experience, familiar with basics like the package and goroutines but eager to level up their server-building skills. We'll cover practical techniques, share real-world examples, and sprinkle in some battle-tested tips from my own projects. Whether you're building a chat app or an IoT data hub, this guide will help you create a robust, scalable TCP server. Let’s get started!
Why Go Rocks for TCP Servers Go is like the Swiss Army knife of network programming: versatile, lightweight, and built for speed. Here’s why it’s the perfect tool for crafting high-performance TCP servers:Goroutines: Lightweight Concurrency Goroutines are like nimble drones, using just a few KB of memory to handle thousands of connections. Unlike heavyweight threads, they let you scale effortlessly.: Spin up a goroutine for each client connection without breaking a sweat.The Package: Your Network Toolkit package is a one-stop shop for TCP programming. No external dependencies needed—just clean, reliable APIs.: Set up a TCP server in under 20 lines of code.Concurrency Primitives: Channels & Think of channels as a conveyor belt, safely passing data between goroutines, while is your traffic light, managing multiple I/O operations.: Use channels to broadcast messages to thousands of clients.Garbage Collection: Stability Without Hassle Go’s optimized garbage collector (improved in Go 1.18+) keeps your server running smoothly, like an invisible cleanup crew.: Run a 24/7 chat server without memory leaks.Cross-Platform Power: Deploy Anywhere Compile your Go server once, and it runs on Linux, Windows, or even a Raspberry Pi—no runtime needed.: Deploy an IoT server to edge devices with zero tweaks.: Want to see these in action? Check out go-redis on GitHub for real-world inspiration!
Segment 2: Core Techniques
Core Techniques for High-Performance TCP Servers Now that we know why Go is awesome, let’s roll up our sleeves and build a TCP server that screams performance. We’ll cover connection management, concurrency optimization, protocol design, and monitoring—complete with code and pitfalls to avoid.
1. Connection Management: Keep the Airport Running A TCP server is like an airport control tower, managing incoming client connections. Go’s and are your tools, but high performance requires finesse.Use and : Listen for connections and handle each in a goroutine.: Set read/write deadlines to prevent zombie connections.: Cap concurrent connections to avoid resource overload.: A simple TCP server.: Without timeouts, idle clients can hog resources. : Use and heartbeat checks.: Run this code and connect using . Send a message and see the response!
2. Concurrency Optimization: Worker Pools to the Rescue Go’s goroutines are great, but creating one per connection can lead to memory issues under high load. Enter the Worker Pool pattern—think of it as a team of workers sharing the workload.: Worker Pool for connections.: Unchecked goroutine creation caused memory spikes in my projects. : Use to monitor and Worker Pools to stabilize.: Have you used Worker Pools in your projects? Share your experience in the comments!
3. Protocol Design: Avoiding Sticky Packets TCP is a stream-based protocol, like a continuous data river. Without clear boundaries, you get sticky packets or fragmentation. A length-prefix protocol is a simple fix.: Length-prefix protocol.: JSON parsing slowed down a chat server I worked on. : Switched to Protobuf, boosting performance by 30%. Try Protobuf for compact, fast serialization.
Segment 3: Real-World Applications
Real-World Applications: From Chat to IoT Let’s see these techniques in action with three scenarios: a chat server, an IoT data collector, and a game server.: Build a server like Discord, handling thousands of users with low-latency messaging.: Store clients in a for broadcasting.: Use channels to distribute messages efficiently.: Limit broadcast goroutines to avoid lock contention.: Broadcasting messages.: Global locks slowed broadcasting. Using a channel-based Worker Pool improved performance by 40%.: Handle thousands of IoT device messages per second, writing them to a database.: Buffer data and write in batches to reduce DB load.: Use channels for non-blocking writes.: Limit DB connections.: Batch processing.: Batch writes reduced database load by 50%. Use for timeout control.: Can you adapt this for a real database like PostgreSQL? Share your code in the comments!
Segment 4: Best Practices and Conclusion
Best Practices for Production-Ready Servers To make your TCP server bulletproof, follow these best practices:Use golang.org/x/sync/semaphore to limit connections.Implement graceful shutdown with .: Graceful shutdown.Use structured logging with or for clear debugging.Leverage for lifecycle management.: Scattered logs made debugging tough. : Use for JSON logs with request IDs.Test with or to measure QPS and latency.High performance, scriptableUse to prevent DDoS attacks.Enable TLS with for secure data transfer.: Unencrypted data was vulnerable. : Add TLS and rotate certificates.Use Docker for consistent environments.Scale with Kubernetes for high availability.: Deployment Options: Manual deployments caused downtime. : Use Docker and Kubernetes for zero-downtime updates.
Wrapping Up: Your Path to TCP Mastery Building a high-performance TCP server in Go is like crafting a well-tuned sports car: it takes the right parts (goroutines, package), careful assembly (connection management, Worker Pools), and constant tuning (profiling, monitoring). Whether you’re creating a chat app, IoT platform, or game server, Go’s simplicity and concurrency model make it a joy to work with.: Build the basic server from Section 3.1 and test with .: Add a Worker Pool and Protobuf for better performance.: Use and Prometheus to track bottlenecks. Go’s ecosystem is evolving fast. Keep an eye on eBPF for kernel-level performance boosts and QUIC for low-latency protocols. Join the Go community on Dev.to or GitHub to share your projects and learn from others!: Have you built a TCP server in Go? Drop your tips or questions in the comments below, and let’s keep the conversation going!

item.title: I Built a Go CLI to Find AWS Cloud Waste & Security Risks in 60 Seconds
item.author: Abdul Herbli
item.content:encoded:
Ever get that sinking feeling when you open the monthly AWS bill? That sense of "death by a thousand cuts" from resources you don't even remember creating?An unattached EBS volume here, an old snapshot there, a forgotten test server over there... Individually, they're small. Together, they are the silent budget killers in the cloud. For years, I've felt the pain of trying to answer two simple questions quickly: and The existing tools were often too complex for this simple task, too expensive, or required granting scary permissions to a third-party service. I wanted something different. I needed a tool that was: I want an answer in seconds, not after a 20-minute setup. One command, one summary. No complex dashboards. It had to run on my machine, using my own credentials, without sending any sensitive data to a server I don't control.After weeks of coding and iteration, the result is a tool I'm excited to share today: .
Introducing NEBU-SCAN: The 60-Second AWS Health Check NEBU-SCAN is a free, single-binary command-line tool written in Go that scans your AWS environment and gives you an instant summary of your most significant cloud waste and critical security issues.It's designed to be the first thing you run when you want a quick health check on your cloud spend and security posture. 💰 Instantly finds costly resources like unattached EBS volumes, idle EC2 instances, and old snapshots that are silently driving up your bill. ⚡️ While looking for waste, it also spots glaring security issues like public S3 buckets, security groups open to the world (), and other common misconfigurations. 🔒 Your AWS credentials . The scan runs locally, and the results are handled with a zero-knowledge model.
Secure by Design: The Zero-Knowledge Report This was the most important part for me. I would never ask a user to trust a new tool with sensitive scan results. So, how do you get a rich, web-based report without uploading sensitive data?The answer is . The CLI runs the scan entirely on your machine. It generates a secret key and encrypts the detailed report uploading. It sends only the encrypted, unreadable data blob to our server. We have zero knowledge of the contents. The CLI gives you a special link containing the secret key. This key lives in the URL fragment (), which is never sent to the server. When you click the link and sign up, your browser fetches the encrypted data and uses the key from the URL to decrypt it locally.We can't see your data. It's secure by design.I've tried to make the installation as simple as possible. It's a single binary with no dependencies.

item.title: ⚡️ GoFr: The Opinionated Go Framework That Just Solved My Boilerplate Problem
item.author: Sourabh Jain
item.content:encoded: Hey crew! If you're building microservices in Go, you know the drill: the language is great, but setting up config, logging, health checks, and tracing can eat up half a day.Let me introduce you to . It's an opinionated, open-source Go microservice framework that just handles all that messy plumbing for you.In short, it lets you focus on your code, not your configuration. You get structured logging, automatic metrics, and instant Kubernetes-ready health checks without lifting a finger.Database & Comms Simplified It has powerful, built-in support for everything from SQL and Redis to Kafka/PubSub—all accessible directly via the Context. It enforces a simple, clean architecture, making scalability and maintenance genuinely easy.If you want a framework that gives you the speed of Go with the convenience of an "" solution, stop manually stitching packages together and check this out.Happy coding! Give the repo a star! ⭐

item.title: Floxy — a lightweight workflow engine for GoLang with saga-style compensation
item.author: Roman Chudov
item.content:encoded:
When building distributed systems or long-running business processes, we often need workflows that can retry, rollback, and recover — without introducing the complexity of Temporal.That’s where Floxy fits in. is a lightweight workflow engine for Go. It provides a simple builder API and a deterministic runtime that supports , , and — all without external dependencies or background daemons.It’s designed to be small, composable, and transparent.Deterministic state machine executionRetry and compensation policiesPartial rollback via save pointsIdempotency control ()Parallel, fork, and join supportEvent-driven persistence for observabilityExecutes its compensation handler .Rolls back previous steps ().Marks the workflow as deterministically.In Floxy, defines the total number of allowed handler calls, including the first one. → one call only (no retries). → up to three total executions.By default, steps are . To mark a step as , use:go Step("charge_card", "ChargeCard", floxy.WithStepNoIdempotent())This ensures the handler runs exactly once, even if it fails.
Deterministic State Model Floxy models workflows as a finite state machine: pending → running → completed failed → compensation → rolled_backEach state transition is explicit and persisted in storage, which makes recovery and auditing reliable and predictable.Transactional workflows with rollback logic (saga pattern)ETL or provisioning pipelinesPayment, booking, and inventory systemsTemporal-like semantics without Temporal’s infrastructureThe repository includes a detailed describing the internal runtime model, retry semantics, and compensation flow.If you find Floxy useful, give it a star and share feedback.Floxy: deterministic workflows for real systems, built the Go way.

item.title: Saving Budget With OpenTelemetry Sampling
item.author: Syukur
item.content:encoded:
On early October 2025, we was doing PoC of using OpenTelemetry exporter for Google Cloud Trace for internal API. The API has been generated 42 millions spans since October 1st - October 6th. Most of spans return status, and we are happy with it. Cloud Trace service is not free. It charges after million spans generated by your application. Since we are not interested with every span generated and captured by Cloud Trace, we was conducting research on OpenTelemetry trace sampling. The purpose is crystal clear - to ingest trace/spans that we are interested into, and In this article, we are going to explore definition of sampling, sampling method for OpenTelemetry trace, how they behave, their implementation, strengths and drawbacks, and their challenges.Oxford dictionary defines sampling as a small amount of a substance taken from a larger amount and tested in order to obtain information about the substance. In observability realm, we can say sampling is a way to gather a group of telemetry data (it could be trace, span, or metrics) where they are represent the whole telemetry data. The main purpose of sampling is to reduce costs without losing visibility.OpenTelemetry mentioned there are situations that you might need samplingApplication generates more than 1000 traces per secondMost of trace data represents healthy traffic with little variationYou are interested in some criteria like errors or high latencyYou have domain-specific criteria you can use to determine relevant data beyon errors and latencyYou have common rules to collect or drop dataYou have a way to tell your application to sample data based on their volumeYou have ability to store unsampled dataYour biggest concern is your budgetIn addition, OpenTelemetry tells there are situation that you might not need samplingYour application generates small data (tens of small traces per second or lower)You only use observability data in aggregate, and can thus pre-aggregate dataYou are bound to regulation where you are not allow to drop dataThere are head sampling and tail sampling to perform OpenTelemetry samplingHead sampling is a sampling technique used to make a sampling decision as early as possible. This decision to sample or drop a trace or span, not inspect trace end-to-endThere are strengths of this sampling technique:Easy to implement and understandCan be done at any point in trace collection pipelineHowever, the primary downside/side effect of this sampling is it is not possible to sample whole trace. For example, you are unable to make sure that you only collect trace with error status in head sampling. For situation or criteria like this, you might need tail sampling.We are going to use these configuration files throughout demonstration of head sampling.A Dockerfile to transform application to Docker Image. We'll use it later alongside Docker Compose0 go build /bin/go-tracing-head-sampling ./tracing-head-sampling/main.go
Docker Compose file to run application and their dependencies. We are going to use OpenTelemetry Collector to ingest traces and spans generated by application, Grafana Tempo to store traces and spans, and Grafana Dashboard to visualize result. We also need a load generator to automatically generate traces with HTTP calls.A script to send HTTP request to application. We will send 10 HTTP requests to server. curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/ 0.2
curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/ 0.2
curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/ 0.2
curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/ 0.2
curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/ 0.2
curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/ 0.2
curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/ 0.2
curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/ 0.2
curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/ 0.2
curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/ 0.2 A configuration file for OpenTelemetry Collector function is the way we done head sampling in Go. It only collects 10% of generated traces. Here is driver code and trace implementationRun the whole application with . Explore Grafana dashboard with Grafana Tempo data source. It might be look like this.Normally, when we don't apply head sampling, we will get 10 traces instead of 1. Since we are using head sampling with 10% generated trace to be sampled, we only get 1 instead of 10.Tail sampling is a way to sample a trace by considering all or part of span within a trace. Tail sampling enables you to choose what to sample and what to drop, based on your interest. You can only sample traces with error status, trace with certain duration, or trace with specific attribute. For more tail sampling configuration, you hereAside from it's strength, there are downsides of implementing tail samplingToday, it is vendor-specific technologyWe are going to use these configuration files throughout demonstration of tail sampling.0 go build /bin/go-tracing-tail-sampling ./tracing-tail-sampling/main.go
We will send 5 HTTP requests to demonstrate how tail sampling works. curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/ 0.2
curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/ 0.2
curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/ 0.2
curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/ 0.2
curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/?fail0.2
curl http://host.docker.internal:9320/ 0.2 Rest of configuration file are same, but the biggest changes lies on OpenTelemetry configuration file and Docker ComposeIn OpenTelemetry config file, we define more specific processor rules instead of having rule. At this moment, we are interested with only traces that return error status. Consequently, no matter how important or numerous spans within a trace as long as they don't return error status, they will We are going to use OpenTelemetry Docker image for OpenTelemetry collector, because image is not supported for tail sampling processor (read more about it here)When accessing Grafana explore with Grafana Tempo data source, we'll get something like thisAmong all requests, our OpenTelemetry collector will only sample trace with error status. In this example, we simulate the error query on HTTP URL.There are several researches regarding OpenTelemetry samplingTail sampling had highest CPU, memory, and network overhead compared to head sampling. This is a trade off for capturing errors.Enabling tail sampling consumes more physical resources compared to disable tail sampling. Consider head sampling for representative trace/span

item.title: Why this simple GO process uses 4 threades?
item.author: achraf DEV
item.content:encoded: Exploring Threads in C vs Go — A Curious Experiment 🧵Recently, I was experimenting with C and Go, and something interesting caught my eye. I wanted to see how many threads each language uses when running a simple program.So, I wrote two nearly identical programs — one in C and one in Go — compiled and ran them. To my surprise, the C program used only 1 thread, while the Go program spun up 4 threads, even though their logic was exactly the same!Here’s what I found and how I tested it 👇

item.title: Markdown Remote Image Downloader
item.author: Alain Airom
item.content:encoded:
Side project for myself 🫠
Motivation on writing this code As an avid blogger, I always strive to maintain local copies of my posts on my laptop. Much like countless other platforms, my content is often crafted in Markdown — a versatile and globally adopted format familiar to anyone who’s encountered a file.A common scenario arises when publishing content online: uploaded images, like the one illustrated above, are typically hosted in remote repositories such as Amazon S3 or similar cloud storage. Consequently, when a blog post is downloaded for local safekeeping, these images are not truly localized; their references within the Markdown still point to external URLs, appearing something like https://some-site/xxx/yyy/1*oqZ8EJKzNONj1FpeHIrebw.png.Simply downloading these images manually and placing them in a local folder, such as /Users/theuser/folder/images/1*oqZ8EJKzNONj1FpeHIrebw.png, introduces a new problem: these are absolute, hard-coded paths. This approach creates a fragile setup; should the parent folder be renamed or moved, these image links will inevitably break, rendering the images unviewable. The robust solution, therefore, is to transform these external URLs into relative references, such as ![](./images/image_name.png), ensuring the images are always found regardless of the document's location within its local directory structure.The idea came to me that it might be nice to be able to do this in an automated way if there is a big number of files which we want to process in a batch way. That is the reason why I wrote a code in Python.Below is the code I wrote in Python which manages to copy embedded images in the “./image” folder under the parent folder and replaces the link to the image in the related Markdown file.While the application operates flawlessly, its performance on a large number of files revealed a significant bottleneck, making it notably slow 🐌.So I wrote a Go application doing the same thing!The performance of this iteration was outstanding; it blazed through the same document set with remarkable speed 💨. However, this entire project is also a deeply personal, recreational pursuit. After what felt like an eternity since my last dive into C development, the urge to revisit and challenge myself with the language became irresistible, culminating in the creation of the final application in C. 👀This one is rocket fast 🚀Again, this is something I’ve been working on for a while, and I just wanted to share it with other geeks who might do non useful stuff as I do sometimes… 😂

item.title: Redpanda in Production: 3 Traps I Fell Into (and How to Avoid Them)
item.author: Pavel Sanikovich
item.content:encoded:
"Redpanda looked like the holy grail — Kafka-compatible, lightning fast, no ZooKeeper, no JVM. Then I ran it in production."Our team runs a high-load B2B marketplace built around event-driven Go microservices. For years, we lived with Kafka’s quirks — and its ZooKeeper demons. Redpanda promised a clean escape: a drop-in replacement, blazing fast, ops-friendly, and written in C++.And while Redpanda delivered on performance, it also delivered a few… surprises. Here are I fell into when running Redpanda in production — and how you can avoid them.
🧩 Trap #1: Default Memory Settings Are Not Your Friend "Redpanda tries to be smart about memory. Until it isn’t."Out of the box, Redpanda auto-tunes memory usage based on your system. That sounds nice — until your production box starts swapping and your broker gets killed by the OOM reaper.In our early tests, Redpanda consumed up to , pushing other processes (like monitoring agents and log collectors) to starvation. Pin your memory limits explicitly.rpk cluster config redpanda.memory.enable_memory_locking rpk cluster config redpanda.memory.available_memory 4G
Auto-tuning is fine for laptops. Not for clusters. Always define available memory explicitly and lock it — otherwise Redpanda take everything it can.
🧬 Trap #2: Schema Evolution Is Still DIY "Kafka-compatible doesn’t mean Kafka-ecosystem-compatible."We used to rely on Confluent Schema Registry for Avro schemas and smooth evolution of message formats. Turns out, Redpanda doesn’t ship with a built-in registry — you need to bring your own., the open-source Schema Registry alternative., which is great for UI inspection but limited for schema enforcement.We ended up hosting Karapace next to Redpanda and managing schema versions manually.Here’s an example Go event we had to version ourselves:No magic migrations. No hidden helpers. Just version your payloads and keep producers + consumers in sync. Redpanda nails the broker layer, but schema management remains your job.
🪞 Trap #3: Monitoring ≠ Kafka Monitoring "Our dashboards were green. Our cluster wasn’t."We reused our existing Prometheus + Grafana dashboards from Kafka, expecting everything to “just work.” Spoiler: metric names differ.under_replicated_partitions — don’t even exist the same way.The result? We thought our system was fine until one broker hit 95% disk usage and silently throttled producers.Use to explore available metrics.Set alerts on , , and .Redpanda has excellent observability — if you wire it up correctly.
⚖️ Bonus Trap: Compatibility ≠ Behavior Parity Kafka clients work, yes — but subtle differences appear under load.For example, in Kafka ensures durability across replicas. In Redpanda, you might hit timeouts under bursty load unless you adjust raft_heartbeat_interval_ms.We also noticed consumer group rebalancing behaving slightly differently. Our fix was simple but crucial — explicitly set the balancer in our Go client: Compatibility ≠ identical semantics. Test consumer lag and rebalance timing under realistic traffic before trusting production.Redpanda remains my top choice for Kafka-compatible workloads. It’s insanely fast, ops-friendly, and spares you from JVM nightmares. But like any sharp tool — it can cut you if you treat it like Kafka 1-to-1.Bring your own schema registry.Validate client behavior.Do that — and you’ll sleep well, even under a million messages per second.Have you deployed Redpanda in production? What traps did fall into? Let’s compare scars in the comments 👇

item.title: TCP Programming in Go: Build Reliable Network Apps with Ease
item.author: Jones Charles
item.content:encoded:
Hey Dev.to community! Ready to build a rock-solid network app that powers chat systems, file transfers, or microservices? That’s where TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) shines—like a trusty courier ensuring your data arrives safely across the internet. And when it comes to TCP programming, is your best friend. With its slick package and lightweight goroutines, Go makes network programming feel like a breeze.This guide is for Go developers with a bit of experience (1-2 years) who want to dive into TCP. We’ll start with the basics, level up with advanced tricks, and build a real-world chat server. Expect clear code, practical tips, and lessons from the trenches. Let’s create something awesome together! 🚀 Go’s concurrency model and standard library let you write high-performance network apps without third-party dependencies. Plus, it’s fun!
1. TCP Programming Basics in Go Let’s kick things off with the essentials: what TCP is, how Go’s package works, and a simple to get your hands dirty.
1.1 What’s TCP All About? Think of TCP as the internet’s reliable delivery service:: No lost packages—TCP retransmits if needed.: Data arrives in the exact order you sent it.: Data flows like a continuous stream, perfect for chats or file transfers.TCP powers web servers, real-time apps like Discord, and IoT systems. Go’s package makes it easy to harness this power.
1.2 Go’s Package: Your Networking Toolkit The package is like a well-stocked toolbox for networking:net.Listen("tcp", ":8080"): Sets up a server to listen for connections.net.Dial("tcp", "localhost:8080"): Connects a client to a server.: The connection object for reading and writing data.Handle each client in a goroutine.
1.3 Build an Echo Server and Client Here’s a quick that listens on port 8080 and sends back whatever the client sends. The client lets you type messages and see the server’s response.Save the server code as and run .Save the client code as and run in another terminal.Type a message in the client, and watch the server echo it back!: Try this on Go Playground or your local machine. Share your tweaks in the comments!
1.4 Avoid These Rookie Mistakes Forgetting to Close Connections: Unclosed can crash your server. Always use .: Check errors from , , and to keep things stable.: Keep protocols simple—newlines work great for starters.
2. Why Go Shines for TCP Programming Go’s like the Swiss Army knife of network programming—simple, reliable, and powerful. Its goroutines, package, and cross-platform support make it a favorite for TCP apps.
2.1 Goroutines: Your Concurrency Superpower Goroutines are Go’s magic trick for handling tons of connections without breaking a sweat. Unlike threads, they’re lightweight and managed by Go’s runtime:: A few KB per goroutine, so you can handle thousands of clients.: Launching is lightning-fast.: Just use the keyword.: In a chat app I worked on, switching from Java threads to Go goroutines cut memory usage by 70%. It was like upgrading to a Tesla!
2.2 The Package: Simple Yet Powerful The package is your go-to for TCP without low-level socket headaches:: handles reading and writing like a champ.: Pair with to cut system calls.: Use to avoid zombie connections.: boosted throughput by 30% in a message queue project!Go’s package works seamlessly on Linux, Windows, and macOS. Just watch for:: Ensure is free.: Tweak on Linux for high concurrency.: Bind to to listen on all interfaces.
2.4 Watch Out for These Traps : Unclosed connections can leave goroutines hanging. Use to clean up.: Without , idle clients can clog your server.Example: Timeout + Context:: Run this and kill it with Ctrl+C—it shuts down gracefully. Share your results with #golang!
3. Advanced TCP Tricks for Go Devs Ready to level up? These advanced practices will help you build production-ready TCP servers.
3.1 Connection Management Done Right Managing connections is like juggling—you need to track clients without dropping the ball:: Reuse connections to save resources.: Close connections cleanly on server stop.:: This server tracks connections and shuts down cleanly. In a log collector project, pooling cut overhead by 40%!
3.2 Smart Data Serialization TCP sends data as a stream, so define message boundaries:: Easy for prototyping.: Compact and fast for production.: Switching to Protobuf in a monitoring system slashed message size by 60%. Try golang/protobuf.
3.3 Error Handling and Logging Don’t let errors crash your app. Use custom errors and for structured logging.Example: Logging with Zap:: logging cut debugging time by 50% in a microservices project. Install with .: reduces system calls.: Cuts latency (conn.(*net.TCPConn).SetNoDelay(true)).: Pools save setup time.: A WebSocket proxy dropped latency from 10ms to 2ms with .: Add to the server above. Share your results with #golang!
4. Real-World TCP Apps You Can Build Let’s see Go’s TCP powers in action with a and quick ideas for file transfers and microservices.
4.1 Build a Real-Time Chat Server Create a mini-Discord backend where users join, chat, and leave. This server tracks users and broadcasts messages.Save as and run .Connect clients with or a custom client.Type a name, send messages, and see them broadcast!: Data races in broadcasting were a headache in a group project. saved the day—always lock shared resources.: Add a command to exit cleanly. Share your version with #golang!
4.2 File Transfers and Microservices More ideas to spark your creativity:: Send files in chunks to save memory. Add resume support for interruptions—our cloud backup project cut recovery time by 80%.Microservices Communication: Use TCP with Protobuf and TLS for secure data sync. TLS eliminated data leak risks in a finance app.Quick File Transfer Snippet:: Add progress tracking to this file transfer. Post your solution in the comments!
5. Wrapping Up: Why Go + TCP Rocks Go makes TCP programming a joyride—simple, fast, and reliable. We covered:: Echo server with the package.: Goroutines, connection pools, and Protobuf for production.: A chat server to flex your skills.Use goroutines for scalabilityBuffer I/O, enable Log errors, manage connectionsTry gRPC for HTTP/2 + Protobuf.Deploy on Kubernetes for cloud-native apps.Build an IoT app—Go’s tiny footprint is perfect for edge devices.: Fire up your editor and build a TCP app! Share it on Dev.to with #golang and #networking, or drop a comment with your ideas. Got questions? The community’s here to help!
6. Resources to Keep Learning Happy coding, and see you in the comments! 🎉

item.title: Como criar uma "busca inteligente" para seu Wiki utilizando Go e ElasticSearch
item.author: José Paulo Marinho
item.content:encoded:
Hoje em dia, existem diversos motores de busca para diferentes finalidades. Como exemplo: o Google. O Google se destaca com técnicas avançadas de rastreamento, indexação e outras tecnologias que tornam suas buscas extremamente eficientes.Neste post, vamos aprender a criar uma busca inteligente para o seu repositório Wiki utilizando arquivos Markdown. Não será tão complexo quanto o Google, mas será rápido, útil e bastante eficiente, utilizando o ElasticSearch.Para começarmos, é necessário que os seguintes softwares estejam instalados e em execução no seu sistema:Observação: neste artigo, não abordaremos como instalar ou configurar esses softwares. Assumimos que eles já estão prontos para uso.Estarei utilizando como repositório o ObsidianNotes - Cycro, um repositório recheado de conhecimento coletado e armazenado em arquivos .Antes de começarmos a implementar, vou explicar como estruturaremos o motor de busca.1 - Leitura dos arquivos Markdown: Percorreremos todos os arquivos do repositório e extrairemos o conteúdo relevante(título, corpo, tags, etc). 2 - Indexação no ElasticSearch - Cada arquivo será transformado em um documento JSON e enviado ao ElasticSearch, que fará a indexação para permitir buscas rápidas e eficientes. 3 - Busca e retorno dos resultados - Implementaremos uma funcão simples que consulta o ElasticSearch com palavras-chave e retorna os arquivos mais relevantes.Certifique-se de ter o Golang instalado e execute os seguintes comandos em um diretório de sua escolha:go-md-search go-md-search go mod init go-md-search Em seguida, vamos instalar o cliente oficial do ElasticSearch para Go:go get github.com/elastic/go-elasticsearch/v9 Podemos criar um script simples que percorre uma pasta e lê todos os arquivos :Note que alguns arquivos podem estar vazios de conteúdo. Para ignora-los, podemos tratar facilmente no Go antes de enviar ao ElasticSearch:
Indexando os arquivos no ElasticSearch Agora que já conseguimos ler e limpar nossos arquivos, o próximo passo é enviar cada documento para o ElasticSearch. Cada arquivo Markdown será representado como um documento , contendo:Note que estabelecemos uma conexão utilizando autenticação básica via usuário e senha. Logo após criamos o índice, caso não haja criado, filtramos os arquivos, ignorando os inválidos e indexamos no índice wiki.Para conferir se o conteúdo foi indexado, execute o seguinte comando com CURL no terminal do seu sistema operacional:http://localhost:9200/wiki/_search?pretty
Realizando buscas no elasticsearch Agora que nossos arquivos Markdown foram indexados no índice , podemos testar buscas diretamente pelo conteúdo. O elasticsearch permite consultas poderosas, não apenas por palavras exatas, mas também por relevância e similaridade.
1. Consulta simples por palavra-chave Podemos criar uma query simples que busca um termo em todos os documentos:No caso estarei buscando somente pelo conteúdo a palavra . - permite buscar em vários campos. - é o termo que o usuário quer encontrar - especifica quais campos do documento serão pesquisados(title, content, etc).
2. Executando a busca em Go Resultados encontrados: 1 - DistributedLocks.md O código exibe quantos documentos foram encontrados e os nomes dos arquivos correspondentes.Para tornar a busca mais inteligente, poderíamos utilizar alguns recursos do Elasticsearch para refinar os resultados, como: Boost por campo(peso aos títulos), Fuzzy search(permite erros de digitação), Highlight(mostra trechos do conteúdo que bateram com a busca).Um exemplo com fuzzy search: - dá peso maior para o campo title - corrige pequenas variações de ou erros de digitaçãoAbaixo encontra-se um programa expondo um servidor web mínimo para executar o que foi aprendido acima:Virá uma resposta como esta:Chegamos ao final de mais um post. Estarei deixando alguns links de referência:

item.title: Mastering JSON the Go Way
item.author: Hugo Oliveira
item.content:encoded: One of the things I love about Go is how practical it is when dealing with JSON. No complicated setup. No magic. Just clear, predictable code.The package provides to work with JSON:- In-memory (Marshal/Unmarshal)- Stream-based (Encoder/Decoder)1. and — for small or simple dataThese are the most common functions when your JSON fits comfortably in memory.You convert a Go struct, map, or slice into JSON.You already have a Go object in memory.You want to send or save it as JSON (HTTP response, file, etc.).The data is not too large (fits easily in memory).You convert JSON bytes into a Go struct or map.You receive JSON as a string or from a file and want to turn it into a Go type.The JSON is fully available in memory.✅ : Always pass a pointer to the destination (), so Go can modify the variable.2. and — for streams or large dataWhen working with or , loading everything into memory isn’t efficient. That’s where and come in — they work with ( and ).▶️Go → JSON stream () You write JSON directly to an output, like a file or HTTP response.You want to write JSON directly to a file, socket, or HTTP response.You want to avoid keeping the full JSON in memory.You’re writing continuous data (e.g., logs, API responses).◀️ JSON stream → Go ()You read JSON progressively from an input, like a file or HTTP request body.You’re reading large JSON data.The data is coming from a network or file stream.You don’t want to load everything at once into memory.Go’s package is a perfect example of the language’s philosophy:simple, explicit, and reliable.When you need something quick and small, use and . When you’re dealing with streams, files, or large payloads, use and .You get both simplicity and performance, and you always know exactly what your code is doing.
